original_hypothesis.title: Retroactive Reward Rate Manipulation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The setRewards function allows governance to modify cantoPerBlock for any epoch, including past epochs. Because update_market is evaluated lazily, changing a past epoch's rate will retroactively alter the reward calculation for any market that has not been updated since that epoch, creating severe economic inconsistencies.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This breaks the immutability of historical economic state. It allows governance to retroactively steal earned rewards or unfairly inflate rewards for specific inactive markets.
preconditions: Governance calls setRewards for an epoch that has already passed
At least one market has a lastRewardBlock prior to the modified epoch

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Governance retroactively lowers rates to erase user debt, or raises rates to enrich insiders in a stale market
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Value is transferred arbitrarily between the protocol treasury and users based on retroactive state changes

economic_questions: Is there any legitimate operational reason to modify past epochs?
How much value can be retroactively altered based on typical update frequencies?
validation_requirements: Simulate retroactive rate reduction and measure impact on pending claims
Verify lack of historical immutability checks

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Variable, depends on the magnitude of the rate change

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a market has been inactive for a long period
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of trust in protocol's economic guarantees

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending rewards in stale markets

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
